mission.title: Install and Configure KubeAI
mission.description: KubeAI is an AI Inference Operator for Kubernetes designed to serve machine learning models, including LLMs, embeddings, and speech-to-text applications. It simplifies the deployment and scaling of ML models while optimizing performance through intelligent load balancing. Key trade-offs include the need for adequate hardware resources and potential complexity in managing model configurations.

steps.title: Add the KubeAI Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the KubeAI Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install KubeAI easily.

```bash

helm repo add kubeai https://github.com/substratusai/kubeai/releases/download/helm-chart-kubeai-0.23.1

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the specific Helm chart repository for KubeAI.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install KubeAI
steps.description:
This step installs KubeAI into your Kubernetes cluster using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace kubeai

helm install kubeai kubeai/kubeai --namespace kubeai --version 0.23.1

```

> Creates a dedicated namespace for KubeAI.
> Installs KubeAI with the specified version in the kubeai namespace.

steps.title: Verify KubeAI installation
steps.description:
This step checks if KubeAI has been successfully deployed by listing the pods in the kubeai namespace.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kubeai

```

> Lists all pods in the kubeai namespace to verify that KubeAI is running.

steps.title: Create an example Custom Resource (CR)
steps.description:
This step creates an example Custom Resource to demonstrate how to use KubeAI for serving a model.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kubeai.io/v1
kind: Model
metadata:
  name: example-model
  namespace: kubeai
spec:
  model:
    name: your-model-name
    version: latest
EOF

```

> Replace 'your-model-name' with the actual model you want to serve.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kubeai.io/v1
kind: Model
metadata:
  name: example-model
  namespace: kubeai
spec:
  model:
    name: your-model-name
    version: latest
EOF

uninstall.title: Uninstall KubeAI
uninstall.description:
This step removes KubeAI from your cluster. Be aware that this will delete all associated resources and data.

```bash

helm uninstall kubeai --namespace kubeai

kubectl delete namespace kubeai

```

> Uninstalls KubeAI from the kubeai namespace.
> Deletes the kubeai namespace and all its resources.

upgrade.title: Upgrade KubeAI
upgrade.description:
This step outlines the process to upgrade KubeAI, including backing up existing configurations and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get models -n kubeai -o yaml > kubeai-backup.yaml

helm upgrade kubeai kubeai/kubeai --namespace kubeai --version 0.23.1

kubectl get pods -n kubeai

```

> Backs up existing model configurations.
> Upgrades KubeAI to the specified version.
> Verifies that the KubeAI pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get models -n kubeai -o yaml > kubeai-backup.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to install CRD'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Custom Resource Definition (CRD) may not have been installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Run 'kubectl apply -f https://github.com/substratusai/kubeai/releases/download/helm-chart-kubeai-0.23.1/kubeai-crds.yaml' to manually install the CRDs.

troubleshooting.title: Pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The application inside the pod may be failing to start due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kubeai' to diagnose the issue.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates a problem with the Kubernetes API server's certificate.

**Fix:**
Ensure your kubeconfig is correctly configured and points to the right cluster context.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in kubeai namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that KubeAI has not been installed correctly or the namespace is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the installation with 'kubectl get pods -n kubeai' and ensure the namespace is correct.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new model types and improved load balancing algorithms.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary: You should see the KubeAI pods running in the kubeai namespace with a status of 'Running'. Use 'kubectl get pods -n kubeai' to confirm.

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the specified resources.
